video_title: How AI is Creating New Millionaires
gist: Joe Lonsdale and founders from Cognition (Devin), coverage (insurance), Joby Aviation, and 8VC partners argue AI capability is doubling every 2-3 months, that diffusion (not model quality) is now the bottleneck, and that foundation-model labs will barbell-attack SaaS from both ends. They contend AI is not a bubble but underhyped, citing modest multiples (Nvidia ~25x forward, Micron 9x) and AI capex at only 3-4% of GDP versus 40-50% in past industrial revolutions.

insights.claim: The METR/'MER' report metric of how much human-equivalent work a model can do autonomously before needing correction has reached roughly 18 hours with Opus 4.6, up from ~10 seconds two-to-three years ago.
insights.speaker: guest (Cognition)
insights.so_what: A concrete, trackable benchmark for autonomy; watch each model release for the doubling to gauge how fast agentic workflows displace human oversight.
insights.quote: I think Opus 4.6 for example, I think was around 18 hours.

insights.claim: The autonomous task-length metric has doubled roughly four-to-five times per year for the last few years, meaning model capability effectively doubles every two-to-three months.
insights.speaker: guest (Cognition)
insights.so_what: Any product or investment thesis with a multi-year horizon must assume roughly an order-of-magnitude capability gain within a year.
insights.quote: you just see it very consistently double. And I think for the last few years, it's doubled about four or five times every year

insights.claim: As autonomy horizons lengthen, the human interaction model shifts from step-by-step code shepherding to assigning whole output-level projects and event-driven, proactive work with no human in the loop each step.
insights.speaker: guest (Cognition)
insights.so_what: Tools built around human-in-the-loop micro-approval will be leapfrogged; the value migrates to orchestration and outcome verification layers.

themes.theme: Software cost collapse and the small-business boom

insights.claim: The cost of building new software is asymptotically dropping to zero, letting a company build a bespoke feature for an enterprise prospect in a single day and close the deal, versus incumbents' three-to-six-month roadmaps.
insights.speaker: guest (Cognition)
insights.so_what: Go-to-market speed becomes a moat; incumbent SaaS that can't build-to-order in days loses deals to AI-native challengers.
insights.quote: the cost of software is just asmtoically dropping to zero of building new software which means that the way you build a company completely changes

insights.claim: AI will drive an explosion in small businesses because it cheaply supplies the specialization (legal gut-checks, financial analysis, software) that previously required a large company's resources.
insights.speaker: guest (Cognition)
insights.so_what: Bullish signal for tools, financing, and services aimed at solo operators and micro-businesses; a new long-tail customer base is forming.
insights.quote: I think there's going to be an explosion in small businesses... AI is actually like an extremely small business enabling technology

insights.claim: The product-engineering-design workflow is shifting from a build-then-learn hypothesis loop toward ingesting company-wide requests into a self-learning AI loop that prototypes and decides what to ship, turning product roles from writers into editors.
insights.speaker: guest (Cognition)
insights.so_what: Prioritization and taste, not implementation, become the scarce skill; org structures optimized for the old loop will lag.
insights.quote: we're a group of wizards who are creating spells that the entire organization can use to make things better

insights.claim: None of Cognition's full-time engineers write code by hand anymore; they work entirely through agents (Codex, Claude Code, Cursor), with the engineering lead running roughly six agents simultaneously and new Linear tickets auto-spawning a dev environment that one-shots a PR for review.
insights.speaker: guest (Cognition)
insights.so_what: A concrete template for AI-native engineering orgs; headcount-per-output economics are being rewritten in real time.
insights.quote: Not a single one of our full-time engineers right now writes code anymore. They do everything through codeex, cloud code, or cursor.

insights.claim: Cognition deliberately hires a high share of former founders, including a 'special projects' team composed entirely of ex-founders, because a problem space this large rewards high-agency people whose only constraint is their own ambition.
insights.speaker: guest (Cognition)
insights.so_what: Signals where elite operator talent is pooling; a hiring model other AI companies may copy to out-compete on execution.

insights.claim: Cognition inverted its interview process to encourage unlimited AI use and instead tests product judgment and trade-off decisions, on the view that screening candidates on tasks AI can already do is the wrong test.
insights.speaker: guest (Cognition)
insights.so_what: A leading indicator for how hiring bars will shift industry-wide; judgment and taste displace raw coding as the evaluated skill.

insights.claim: Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google will scan where their tokens are heavily used and directly attack those high-value application surfaces, so the top 1% of SaaS is as at risk as the low end.
insights.speaker: guest (coverage/JD Ross)
insights.so_what: Reframes the risk: not just cheap SaaS but the most valuable verticals are targets; token-usage concentration is a map of where labs will build next.
insights.quote: Where's a lot of tokens being used? Great. Let's go after and like attack that surface.

insights.claim: The AI value structure is a barbell: large foundation-model companies capture the top-end value while everyone in the middle commoditizes, and within five years customers will one-shot better bespoke replacements for most vertical SaaS.
insights.speaker: guest (coverage/JD Ross)
insights.so_what: Mid-market SaaS multiples are structurally threatened; defensibility lives at the model layer or in non-software trust businesses.
insights.quote: the large AI companies are going to eat the top end of value and everyone in the middle is going to just commoditize

insights.claim: Low-end SaaS stocks and their investors were crushed the prior week after a new Anthropic release stoked fears the products can be rebuilt.
insights.speaker: Joe Lonsdale
insights.so_what: Model-release cadence is now a direct catalyst for repricing software equities; track lab launches as market-moving events.
insights.quote: all these SAS companies the lowend ones got crushed last week

insights.claim: Defensible AI businesses are those selling 'unknown unknowns' to buyers like CFOs and general counsel—risk management and insurance—where trusted expertise, not software features, is the product.
insights.speaker: guest (coverage/JD Ross)
insights.so_what: A screen for AI-resilient business models: trust and consequence-bearing advisory verticals survive commoditization better than pure tooling.

insights.claim: Software-adjacent AI startups sit in a war zone with the labs, so competing there requires elite talent (Lonsdale is bullish on Cognition for having ~20 Olympiad gold medalists) and most teams without that caliber should not attempt it.
insights.speaker: Joe Lonsdale
insights.so_what: Filters which AI-application bets are viable; capital should favor either non-software-adjacent verticals or truly world-class technical teams.
insights.quote: if you don't have 20 other global gold medal physics and math leaders around, you probably shouldn't be bothering to compete in that area

insights.claim: New lab features arrive fast enough to erase whole business functions overnight—Claude 'cowork' (released ~two weeks prior) now automates carrier-portal document ingestion at coverage, a task no employee works on anymore.
insights.speaker: guest (coverage/JD Ross)
insights.so_what: Illustrates how quickly labor cost is stripped out of ops; back-office automation is compounding faster than most enterprises budget for.
insights.quote: It's an entire entire area of our business that now is automated that zero people work on.

themes.theme: Joby Aviation: autonomy, hydrogen, and defense

insights.claim: Joby's hydrogen fuel-cell propulsion delivers roughly a 6x energy advantage over jet fuel (3x higher specific energy plus 2x conversion efficiency), and Joby has already flown a hydrogen-electric VTOL more than 500 miles.
insights.speaker: JoeBen Bevirt
insights.so_what: If validated at scale, hydrogen could redefine aircraft range economics; a differentiated moat versus battery-electric eVTOL competitors.
insights.quote: the hydrogen's three times lighter than jet fuel... And then we can convert it into propulsion twice as efficiently. So you get a 6x gain.

insights.claim: Joby is teasing a non-VTOL, longer-range hydrogen aircraft and expects hydrogen propulsion to eventually redefine every class of aircraft, with strong Department of War interest driven by dramatically lower cost and unprecedented performance.
insights.speaker: JoeBen Bevirt
insights.so_what: Signals a defense revenue vector beyond air-taxi; a hydrogen platform competing with rotorcraft like the Blackhawk expands Joby's TAM.

insights.claim: Joby uses Nvidia's latest-generation autonomous chipsets and safety-autonomy stack (built for driving and robotics) in its aircraft, positioning autonomy as a pilot backup that could eventually remove the legally required pilot.
insights.speaker: JoeBen Bevirt
insights.so_what: Ties Joby to Nvidia's autonomy supply chain; regulatory approval of pilotless operation is the key value-unlock catalyst to watch.

insights.claim: Joby claims to be the world's most vertically integrated aviation company, designing and manufacturing more than 90% of aircraft components in-house, from autonomy computers and batteries to motors, propellers, and airframe.
insights.speaker: JoeBen Bevirt
insights.so_what: Vertical integration compounds AI productivity gains across the stack; a structural cost and iteration-speed argument for the equity.

insights.claim: AI has made Joby's chief aerodynamicist self-reportedly 10x more productive, letting him build in an afternoon what previously took weeks or months and clear a years-long backlog of aircraft-design projects.
insights.speaker: JoeBen Bevirt
insights.so_what: Evidence that AI productivity gains extend into hard-science R&D, not just software; accelerates physical-world innovation timelines.
insights.quote: He's now able to build uh in an afternoon what would have previously taken him weeks or months... I'm 10x as productive as I was just a few months ago.

insights.claim: The panel argues AI is not a bubble and is arguably underhyped, citing Nvidia at ~25x forward earnings and Micron up 250% over 12 months yet still trading at a ~9x forward PE.
insights.speaker: guest (8VC partner)
insights.so_what: The 'no multiple dislocation' argument implies more upside if earnings keep compounding; a counter to bubble-thesis positioning.
insights.quote: Micron... The stock is up 250% over the last 12 months and it was a nine times... forward multiple stock 12 months ago and it's still a nine times forward PE stock today.

insights.claim: AI capex is around a trillion dollars but only ~3-4% of GDP, versus 20-25% of GDP in early-20th-century Russia and 40-50% in China's industrial buildout, implying spending could get far larger if AI is a true industrial revolution.
insights.speaker: guest (8VC partner)
insights.so_what: Frames current investment as early-stage; supports a longer, larger capex cycle for infrastructure, compute, and memory suppliers.
insights.quote: in AI we're investing like 3% or 4%. It's like it's not even yet on the scale of the industrial revolution.

insights.claim: Technology is no longer the bottleneck—diffusion into the economy is—yielding roughly 4% productivity growth in affected areas and a slow ~10-year revolution rather than an instant shock, so the direction is right but timing is uncertain.
insights.speaker: guest (8VC partner)
insights.so_what: Tempers timing expectations; the trade is durable but you can be right on the trend and still lose money by mistiming magnitude or timeline.
insights.quote: the limiting factors now are not the technology, it's diffusion of the technology into the economy

insights.claim: Law will be transformed from the bottom up because it maps onto four LLM-strong skills (reading, searching, writing documents, some reasoning), forcing a shift from per-hour billing to outcome-based pricing.
insights.speaker: guest (8VC partner)
insights.so_what: A whole professional-services business model is due to reprice; watch legal-tech and outcome-priced service startups as the transition plays out.
insights.quote: hours don't make any sense when it's agents doing work... You have to shift now to fe like pricing outcomes.

insights.claim: Macro commentators are missing the productivity story because they are too far from the ground and not talking to the small teams actually using tools like Claude Code, and are demographically older and slower to adopt.
insights.speaker: Joe Lonsdale
insights.so_what: Suggests a persistent information edge for hands-on practitioners over traditional macro analysis when positioning around AI.
insights.quote: the macro people are so far away from the practical reality on the ground. They're not talking to 5, 10, 15 person teams who are actually doing this stuff.

insights.claim: ZIRP-era froth is returning in Silicon Valley, with companies being re-marked at 5x valuation just two months later on minimal progress, a pattern the speaker directly witnessed in the 2021-22 peak cycle.
insights.speaker: guest (8VC partner)
insights.so_what: Localized bubbles exist even if public multiples look sane; late-stage private AI rounds warrant valuation discipline.
insights.quote: two months later they would be investing in the same business with two months of progress at 5x the valuation

insights.claim: Technology is more cyclical than credited because many startups are indexed to serving other tech startups, so a venture downturn cascades into the vendors selling tools to that cohort—meaning you must be careful what customer base you index to.
insights.speaker: guest (8VC partner)
insights.so_what: A diligence lens for AI-app investing: revenue concentrated in other startups amplifies drawdown risk during corrections.
insights.quote: you have to be careful what you index to

insights.claim: The primary risk over the next decade is social dynamics and job displacement, with the danger that populist politicians on both right and left seize management of the transition and mismanage it, rather than a technology failure.
insights.speaker: guest (8VC partner)
insights.so_what: Policy and political backlash, not tech, are the tail risk; monitor regulation and labor politics as the main threat to the AI thesis.
